volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: Pseudolus (Section 4.7.1229 - Section 5.2.1322)

sections.label: Section 4.7.1229
sections.original: Quid nunc faciam ?
sections.translation: What am I to do then ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1229b
sections.original: Si mi argentum dederis , te suspendito .
sections.translation: When you have given me the money — go hang yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1230
sections.original: Di te perdant . sequere sis me ergo hac ad fórum , ut solvam .
sections.translation: The Gods confound you . ( To HARPAX .) Follow me , then , this way , please , to the Forum , that I may pay you .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1230b
sections.original: Sequor .
sections.translation: I follow you .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1231
sections.original: Quid ego ?
sections.translation: What am I to do ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1231b
sections.original: Peregrinos absolvam , cras agam cum civibus .
sections.translation: Strangers I ’ ll pay at once ; to-morrow I ’ ll settle with fellow-citizens . Pseudolus has been holding a council-general [*] against my life , in sending that fellow to me to-day to carry off the woman . ( To HARPAX .) Do you follow me , ( To the AUDIENCE .) Now don ’ t you be expecting that I shall be returning home this way .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1235
sections.original: ita res gestast : angiporta haec certum est consectarier .
sections.translation: As matters stand , I ’ ve determined to go through the alleys .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1236
sections.original: Si graderere tantum quantum loquere , iam esses ad forum .—
sections.translation: If you had walked at the rate you talk , you ’ d by this have been at the Forum .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1237
sections.original: Certumst mi hunc emortualem facere ex natali die .—
sections.translation: I ’ m determined to make this , instead of my birthday , my dying day .

sections.label: Section 4.8.1238
sections.original: Bene ego illum tetigi , bene autem servos inimicum suom .
sections.translation: I ’ ve touched this fellow handsomely , and cleverly has my servant managed his adversary . Now am I resolved to lie in ambush for Pseudolus

sections.label: Section 4.8.1240
sections.original: quam in aliis comoediis fit , ubi cum stimulis aut flagris
sections.translation: in a different manner to what ’ s done in other plays , where people lie in wait with goads or whips . Without revenge will I at once pay down the twenty minae which I promised if he should effect it . I ’ ll carry them to him of my own accord . This creature is very clever , very cunning , very artful . Pseudolus has surpassed the Trojan stratagem [*] and Ulysses too .

sections.label: Section 4.8.1245
sections.original: nunc ibo intro , argentum promam , Pseudolo insidias dabo .—
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll be off in-doors . I ’ ll take out the money ; I ’ ll lie in ambush for Pseudolus .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1246
sections.original: Quid hóc ? sicine hóc fit , pedés ? statin án non ?
sections.translation: How ’ s this ? And is it the fact ? Feet-are you standing or not ? Or is it this you want , some ; one to pick me up here as I lie ? But , by my faith , if I do fall down , yours will be the fault . Are you going to go ? Heigho ! I must wait upon myself .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1250
sections.original: hodie ést ; magnum hoc vitiúm vino est :
sections.translation: This is the great fault in wine ; it first lays hold of the feet ; ’ tis a cunning wrestler . By my faith , assuredly am I now come off right well drenched ; with such exquisite viands , with such becoming elegance , in such a delightful place , have we been delightfully entertained .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1255
sections.original: quid opúst me multas agere ámbages ? hoc
sections.translation: What ’ s the need for me to make much prosing ? This is the thing for a man , an object for him to pass his life for ; here are all pleasures and all delights . I think that the ecstasy is equal to that of the Deities , when the lover embraces his mistress , when he places lip to lip ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.1260
sections.original: úbi alter alterúm bilingui mánifesto inter sé prehendunt ,
sections.translation: when melting kisses are exchanged , when breast is pressed to breast , or else , if they please , they are locked in strict embrace ; then for your most loving mistress , with her white hand , to be pledging you in the luscious goblet , for no one there to be disagreable to another , for no one to be indulging in silly conversation ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.1265
sections.original: unguénta atque odóres , lemníscos , coróllas
sections.translation: for unguents and perfumes , ribbons [*] and festive wreaths , to be provided in profusion ; and for the rest of the entertainment , too , to be provided in no niggardly style . That no one may have to question me then , in this manner have myself and my young master been spending this day in jollity .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1269_1270
sections.original: póstquam opus meum ómne ut volui pérpetravi hóstibus fugatis .
sections.translation: After I had fulfilled all my task just as I intended , the enemy put to flight , I was leaving them reclining and drinking , each lover with his mistress , and my own mistress there as well , indulging heart and soul . But after I had risen , they begged me to dance . After this fashion ( he dances ) did I show myself off there quite charmingly , in a master-like style , to wit ; for I am

sections.label: Section 5.1.1275
sections.original: probe Ionica perdidici . sed palliolatim amictus
sections.translation: thoroughly acquainted with the Ionian step . Thus , clad in my little mantle , full of fun , I was stepping about , this way . Some of them clapped me , others cried out for me to dance again . In that same Ionian fashion once again did I begin to take a turn ; I presented myself to my mistress ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.1278
sections.original: út me amarét : ubi círcumvortór , cado :
sections.translation: that she might caress me ; as I was pirouetting , down I tumbled : that was the funeral dirge [*] for my sport . And so , while I was a-struggling to get up , near —, almost , I mean , I soiled my mantle .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1280
sections.original: nímiae tum volúptati edepol
sections.translation: Then , by my troth , I was the cause of plenteous mirth . A goblet was presented me on account of my fall . Forthwith I changed my mantle , and put on this ; thence have I come hither , that I might get rid of my surfeit . Now I ’ m going to my old master , to put him in mind of our bargain . Open-open the door . Hallo , there ! Tell Simo , somebody , that I ’ m here .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1285
sections.original: Vóx viri péssumi me éxciet foras .
sections.translation: The voice of a rascally fellow is calling me out of doors . ( He stares at PSEUDOLUS .) But what ’ s this ? How ’ s this ? What is it I see in this guise ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1287
sections.original: Cúm corona ébrium Pseúdolum tuom .
sections.translation: Your own Pseudolus , drunk , with a chaplet on [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1288
sections.original: Líbere hercle hóc quidem . séd vide statum .
sections.translation: By my troth , this is free and easy indeed . But see his attitude ; is he on my account a bit the more afraid ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1290
sections.original: cógito , saéviter blanditerne ádloquar .
sections.translation: I ’ m thinking whether I shall address him harshly or kindly . But this ( pointing to a purse in his hand ) that I ’ m carrying forbids me to use rough measures towards him just now ; if there ’ s any hope for me , centred in this .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1293
sections.original: Vír malus viro óptumo obviam it .
sections.translation: A worthless fellow is coming to meet the best of men .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1294
sections.original: Dí te ament , Pseúdole . fú i in malám crucem .
sections.translation: May the Gods bless you , Pseudolus . ( PSEUDOLUS eructates .) Foh ! go to utter perdition .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1295
sections.original: Cur égo adflictor ?
sections.translation: But why should I have that mischance befal me ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1295b
sections.original: Quid tú , malum , in os igitúr mi ebrius inrúctas ?
sections.translation: Why , the plague , in your drunkenness , are you eructating in my face ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1296
sections.original: Mólliter síc tene mé , cave né cadam :
sections.translation: Hold me up , steadily ; take care that I don ’ t fall . Don ’ t you see me , how drenched and soaking I am ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1298
sections.original: Quae ístaec audáciast , té sic intérdius
sections.translation: What impudence is this , for you to be going about this way in broad daylight , drunk , with a chaplet on ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1299b
sections.original: Lubet .
sections.translation: Such is my pleasure . ( Eructates again .)

sections.label: Section 5.2.1300
sections.original: Quíd , lubet ? pérgin ructáre in os mihi ?
sections.translation: Why your pleasure ? Do you persist in eructating in my very face ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1301
sections.original: Suávis ructús mihi est . síc sine , Simo .
sections.translation: An eructation is comforting to me ; do indulge me in it ; do but stand off .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1302
sections.original: Credo équidem potesse té , scelus ,
sections.translation: For my part I really do believe , you villain , that you are able in a single hour to drink up four right plentiful vintages of the Massic hills [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1304b
sections.original: Hiberna áddito .
sections.translation: A winter hour [*] , add .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1305
sections.original: Hau mále mones , sed díc tamen ,
sections.translation: You don ’ t remind me amiss . But tell me , however , whence I am to say that you are bringing your deeply-laden bark ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1307
sections.original: Cúm tuo fílio pérpotaví modo .
sections.translation: I ’ ve just been having a thorough bout with your son .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1308
sections.original: séd , Simo , ut probe táctus Ballio est !
sections.translation: But , Simo , wasn ’ t Ballio nicely diddled ? How well I carried what I told you into effect .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1310
sections.original: Péssumu ’ (s e) s homo .
sections.translation: You are a most worthless fellow .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1310b
sections.original: Mulier haec facit .
sections.translation: That damsel is the cause of this ; along with your son she is carousing , a free woman .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1312
sections.original: ómnia , ut quícque egisti , órdine scio .
sections.translation: I know everything in its order , just as you managed each particular .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1313
sections.original: Quíd ergo dubitas dáre mi argentum ?
sections.translation: Why , then , do you hesitate to pay me the money ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1313b
sections.original: Iús petis , fateór . tene .
sections.translation: You ask what ’ s just , I confess ; take it .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1314
sections.original: át negabás daturum ésse te mihi .
sections.translation: But you declared that you wouldn ’ t give it me ; and still do you give it .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1315
sections.original: onera húnc hominem atque me cónsequere hac .
sections.translation: Load your shoulder with it , and follow me this way .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1315b
sections.original: Egone ístum onerém ?
sections.translation: I — load myself with that ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1315c
sections.original: Onerábis , scio .
sections.translation: You will load yourself , I ’ m sure .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1316
sections.original: Quid ego huíc homini faciám ? satin ultro et argéntum aufert et me ínridet ?
sections.translation: What am I to do to this fellow ? Doesn ’ t he , contrary to my expectation , take my money , and then laugh at me ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1317
sections.original: Vae víctis .
sections.translation: Woe to the conquered [*] :

sections.label: Section 5.2.1317b
sections.original: Vorte ergo úmerum .
sections.translation: turn your back , then .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1318
sections.original: Em .
sections.translation: Well ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.1318b
sections.original: Hóc ego numquam rátus sum
sections.translation: I really didn ’ t suppose that it would ever come to pass with me that I should be begging of you .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1320
sections.original: heu heú heu .
sections.translation: Oh ! oh !

sections.label: Section 5.2.1320b
sections.original: Desine .
sections.translation: desist . Let me alone —

sections.label: Section 5.2.1320c
sections.original: Dóleo .
sections.translation: I ’ m in pain .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1320d
sections.original: Ni doléres tu , ego dolérem .
sections.translation: Were you not in pain , I should be in pain ;

sections.label: Section 5.2.1321
sections.original: Quid ? hoc aúferen , Pseudole mi , aps tuo ero ?
sections.translation: What ? Are you going to take this from your master , Pseudolus ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.1321b
sections.original: Lubentíssimo corde atque ánimo .
sections.translation: With most willing heart and soul .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1322
sections.original: Non aúdes , quaeso , aliquám partem mihi grátiam facere hinc árgenti ?
sections.translation: Prithee , can ’ t you venture to make me an abatement of some portion of this money ?
